verses.note: HEBREW: The book opens in Hebrew. The 'third year of Jehoiakim' (ca. 605 BC) presents a well-known chronological question -- Jeremiah 25:1 calls this Jehoiakim's fourth year. The resolution is that Daniel uses the Babylonian system (which does not count the accession year), while Jeremiah uses the Judean system. Both point to the same date.

verses.note: RENAMING: The Babylonians renamed the Hebrew youths to overwrite their theological identity. Daniel ('God is my judge') becomes Belteshazzar ('Bel protect his life'); Hananiah ('YHWH is gracious') becomes Shadrach (possibly 'command of Aku'); Mishael ('who is what God is?') becomes Meshach (possibly 'who is what Aku is?'); Azariah ('YHWH has helped') becomes Abed-nego ('servant of Nebo'). The renaming failed -- Daniel's God proved supreme.

verses.flow: You watched until a stone was cut out without hands, and it struck the image on its feet of iron and clay, and crushed them.
verses.note: CHRIST: The stone 'cut out without hands' (even gizeret di la bidayin) -- this is a kingdom NOT of human origin. No human agency establishes it. It is supernatural, divine, and it destroys all earthly empires not by reform but by catastrophic replacement. Christ identified His kingdom with this stone (Matt 21:44; Luke 20:18). The phrase 'without hands' emphasizes divine, non-human agency -- the same concept behind the virgin birth, the resurrection, and the Second Coming.

verses.note: CHRIST/DIVINE COUNCIL: 'The appearance of the fourth is like a son of the gods' (bar elahin). Nebuchadnezzar, a pagan king, recognizes a divine being in the fire. In v.28 he calls this figure God's 'angel' (malakh). The Aramaic 'bar elahin' literally means 'a son of the gods' -- in the Babylonian framework this would be a member of the divine council. In the biblical framework, this is a Christophany -- a pre-incarnate appearance of the Son of God. The same one who walks with His people in fire is the one who descended into death and rose again. Compare Isaiah 43:2: 'When you walk through the fire, you shall not be burned.'

verses.note: CHRIST: 'One like a son of man' (ke-var enash) -- THE defining messianic title. In Aramaic, 'bar enash' simply means 'a human being,' but this figure 'comes with the clouds of heaven' -- cloud-riding is exclusively a divine prerogative in the Old Testament (Psalm 68:4; 104:3; Isaiah 19:1). This figure is therefore both human ('like a son of man') and divine (cloud-rider). He approaches the Ancient of Days and receives eternal, universal dominion. Jesus adopted this title as His primary self-designation (used ~80 times in the Gospels). At His trial, Jesus explicitly combined Daniel 7:13 with Psalm 110:1: 'You will see the Son of Man seated at the right hand of Power and coming with the clouds of heaven' (Mark 14:62). The high priest tore his robes because he understood exactly what Jesus was claiming: identity with this divine-human figure who receives worship from all nations.

verses.flow: Seventy weeks are decreed about your people and your holy city, to finish the transgression, to put an end to sin, and to atone for iniquity, to bring in everlasting righteousness, to seal both vision and prophet, and to anoint a most holy place.
verses.note: CHRIST: The Seventy Weeks prophecy (shavu'im shiv'im) -- the most detailed messianic timeline in the Old Testament. HEBREW: 'shavu'im' means 'sevens' or 'weeks' (not necessarily weeks of days). Seventy sevens = 490 units. The six purposes listed are: (1) to finish transgression, (2) to put an end to sin, (3) to atone for iniquity, (4) to bring in everlasting righteousness, (5) to seal vision and prophet, (6) to anoint a most holy place. These are comprehensively fulfilled in the work of Christ -- His atonement (items 1-3) and His inaugurated kingdom (items 4-6). The phrase 'to anoint a most holy' (limshoch qodesh qodashim) may refer to a most holy place or a Most Holy One.

verses.flow: Know therefore and understand that from the going out of the word to restore and build Jerusalem to the coming of an anointed one, a prince, there shall be seven weeks. Then for sixty-two weeks it shall be built again with squares and moat, but in a troubled time.
verses.note: CHRIST: 'From the going out of the word to restore and build Jerusalem to the coming of an anointed one, a prince' (ad mashiach nagid). The Hebrew 'mashiach' = anointed one = Messiah = Christ. 'Nagid' = prince, ruler, leader. Seven weeks + sixty-two weeks = 69 weeks (483 years). If counted from the decree of Artaxerxes to Nehemiah (445 BC, Neh 2:1-8) using prophetic years of 360 days, this reaches precisely to ~AD 32 -- the time of Christ's public ministry. The precision of this prophecy is staggering.

verses.note: CHRIST: 'An anointed one shall be cut off and shall have nothing' (yikkaret mashiach ve-ein lo). HEBREW: 'karat' means to cut off, to cut a covenant (by blood), to execute. The Messiah is 'cut off' -- killed -- and 'has nothing' (or: 'not for himself'). This is the crucifixion of Jesus Christ, who died not for His own sins but for the sins of the world. 'The people of the prince who is to come shall destroy the city and the sanctuary' -- fulfilled in AD 70 when the Roman legions under Titus destroyed Jerusalem and the Second Temple. 'Its end shall come with a flood' -- overwhelming destruction. Jesus cited this passage in the Olivet Discourse (Matt 24:15).

verses.flow: At that time shall arise Michael, the great prince who has charge of your people. And there shall be a time of trouble, such as never has been since there was a nation till that time. But at that time your people shall be delivered, everyone whose name shall be found written in the book.
verses.note: ESCHATOLOGY: 'Michael, the great prince' (mikhael ha-sar ha-gadol) -- Michael's role is to protect Israel in the ultimate crisis. 'A time of trouble such as never has been' -- Jesus quotes this phrase in the Olivet Discourse (Matt 24:21). 'Everyone whose name is found written in the book' -- the book of life (cf. Rev 20:12, 15; Phil 4:3). Deliverance is not universal but particular: it belongs to those inscribed by God.

verses.flow: And many of those who sleep in the dust of the earth shall awake, some to everlasting life, and some to shame and everlasting contempt.
verses.note: RESURRECTION: 'Many of those who sleep in the dust of the earth shall awake' -- the clearest Old Testament statement of bodily resurrection. HEBREW: 'yaqitsu' (shall awake) -- the metaphor of death as sleep implies waking. 'Some to everlasting life' (le-chayyei olam) -- this phrase appears only here in the Hebrew Bible. 'Some to shame and everlasting contempt' (le-charafot le-dir'on olam) -- a double outcome: resurrection is universal, but destiny diverges. Jesus alludes to this in John 5:28-29: 'All who are in the tombs will hear his voice and come out -- those who have done good to the resurrection of life, and those who have done evil to the resurrection of judgment.'

verses.flow: And those who are wise shall shine like the brightness of the heavenly expanse, and those who turn many to righteousness, like the stars forever and ever.
verses.note: GLORY: 'Those who are wise shall shine' (ha-maskilim yazhiru) -- the 'maskilim' are the wise teachers of 11:33-35 who instruct others even during persecution. They shall shine 'like the brightness of the expanse' (ke-zohar ha-raqia) -- the same word 'zohar' gives the great Jewish mystical text its name. 'Like the stars forever and ever' -- Jesus echoes this in Matthew 13:43: 'Then the righteous will shine like the sun in the kingdom of their Father.' The wise become luminaries in God's eternal kingdom.

verses.flow: But go your way till the end. And you shall rest and shall stand in your allotted place at the end of the days.
verses.note: CONCLUSION: 'You shall rest and shall stand in your allotted place at the end of the days' (ta'amod le-goralekha le-qets ha-yamim). Daniel is promised rest (death) and then resurrection ('you shall stand') at the appointed end. His 'lot' or 'allotted place' (goral) is his inheritance in the age to come. This is the Old Testament hope of resurrection -- not mere survival of the soul, but bodily standing at the end of days. The book ends not with apocalyptic terror but with personal promise: Daniel, you will rise.
